lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: So — let me ask you the question this whole paper is built on. What is education for, when the shape of it has outlived the reason we built it?

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: That is a big opening. But I think it's the honest one. Because for most of the last century, we had a pretty clear answer — we had factories to staff. We needed workers who could follow instructions, read simple manuals, keep a schedule. And schools got built to produce exactly that.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: Age-graded rooms, fifty-minute periods, separated subjects, letter grades. All of that wasn't some deep truth about how learning works. It was the shape that fit the moment.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: Right. And now the moment is gone. [sighs] The economic argument — learn this content because you'll need it for your job — has collapsed, because AI can do a lot of that cognitive work already. So what's left?

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: That's the thing that struck me most reading this paper. They don't pretend to know the final answer. They don't even pretend to be building the new system. They call it, explicitly — a first honest try after the rethinking.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: Which, by the way, is a surprisingly rare framing. Most education reform shows up with a system to sell.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: Yeah. This one shows up with a thesis — silos are a cognitive convenience, nature never agreed to them — and then a set of conditions the authors think a real answer would have to satisfy.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: And a lot of humility about being wrong. There's a line in there — [slight pause] when every inherited truth is shaking at once, the responsible response is not to manufacture a new certainty. It's to stop, think, and try something.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: We're going to walk through the whole argument in this series. Nine parts. Crisis, why reforms keep failing, the theoretical foundation with Sapolsky, Rogers, Robinson — plus Dewey, Vygotsky, Freire — then the eight principles, three levels of learning, the political dimension, and what we think is actually being built right now.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: Each episode stands on its own. But they add up. If you're in a hurry, jump to whichever section bit you most — and come back for the rest when you have a walk.
